### Passage:
The hawk soared high above the meadow, his keen eyes scanning the landscape below. He was curious why the small, furry creatures—a species known as ground squirrels—always scurried away when he approached. The hawk didn’t understand why the ground squirrels didn’t want to be his friend.

Every day, the hawk would swoop down, hoping to engage with the squirrels, but they'd disappear into their burrows or dash under the tall grasses, leaving him to land in the empty fields. Their behavior puzzled him greatly. The squirrels' reactions seemed counterintuitive to him, as they would scamper off the moment he appeared, his wings cutting through the air.

If I don’t like something, I’ll stay away from it. Such a simple concept drove most of his daily decisions. The hawk didn’t need the validation or companionship of the ground squirrels but couldn’t grasp why they shunned him so vigorously. Being a creature of habit and practicality, he didn’t let their behavior sway him.

Yet beneath his stoic exterior, there was always an idea bubbling under the surface. A change that would help him make a difference and not have to worry about how people or other animals judged him. He dreamed of leaving his law firm to open a portable dog wash.

The hawk didn’t understand why the ground squirrels didn’t want to be his friend. They seemed to have no reason for their fears despite how he tried to socialize.

Every time the hawk attempted to get close to them, they'd vanish in a flash, disappearing beneath the earth. He couldn't understand why they were always so afraid. He had tried smiling, dancing and even tried singing soft tunes to prove his friendly nature, yet the ground squirrels would scamper away. This didn't even make sense, the hawk thought. If I don’t like something, I’ll stay away from it.

The hawk had a unique background. By day, he worked at a prestigious law firm, where people often described him as a keen observer, meticulous and precise, much like the description of the work he did. However, while colleagues and clients admired him they kept telling him he had to see things as "orange." He just didn’t see it to be orange. Pink was his colour. The hawk found it hard to align himself with people talking about the same things, and felt his life was lacking something meaningful. He never understood what the hawk was to these people, it didn’t align with what he saw or how he saw himself. He looked like an eagle, but acted and thought more like a hawk.

Despite his illustrious career, the hawk yearned for something more, an escape from the mundane routines of legal briefs and courtrooms. He dreamed of leaving his law firm to open a portable dog wash and find happiness. It didn’t have to be a lot of cash money, he would be able to help those who love their dogs. He could find meaning in how much the clients would love their dogs back more after going to his business. He’d be able to see some smiles, and not the fearful faces of the ground squirrels. The hawk didn’t understand why the ground squirrels didn’t want to be his friend.

You realize you're not alone as you sit in your bedroom massaging your calves after a long day of playing tug-of-war with Grandpa Joe in the hospital. The muscles in your legs ache from the exertion, but the memory of Grandpa's laughter, no matter how weak, puts a smile on your face. His spirit endures despite the confines of his hospital bed. There are many things Grandpa had taught him and it would all be lost if you were not there to hear the stories and laughter. This shared time has a way of making you stronger inside, much like his feeble arms had done. Joe discovered that traffic cones make excellent megaphones.
I often wonder if the bond we share with our grandparents is different from the regular one between children and parents. I’m starting to worry that my pet turtle could tell what I was thinking, the way Mom used to know when I was hiding something. The turtle's wisdom seems beyond his years. There’s a certain comfort in the silence of the turtle, a serenity that I find myself seeking out with these long and drawn out minutes in a quiet hallway. I would tell him my fears and my hopes. My secret thoughts were always kept safe with him as he would occasionally move his head side to side as if listening to every word. It is no surprise to me Joe's turtle loves making people laugh. He's in a boy band which doesn't make much sense for a snake.
Joe had walked into the hospital room, cones hanging from around his neck as if they were part of the uniform he always seemed to be wearing. He always had a way of turning everyday objects into something special, something memorable. I always knew he and Grandpa would have stories and laughter all day long if he had the opportunity to play his cone shaped instruments. But if you close your eyes, you could nearly see the traffic cone in place of the megaphone they were using. Just him and his Grandpa had a sense of fun no matter what the circumstances.
